HYBRID_OVERVIEW.md
v_csr_plan.md

1 # How the Hybrid Simulator Works 2 3 A friendly tour for engineers new to this codebase. 4 5 ## The Problem in One Paragraph 6 7 A pure RTL simulator (`vsim`, built on Verilator + SystemC) is **cycle-accurate**: it tells you exactly how many cycles every instruction takes inside the CPU pipeline. The price is speed: it runs at ~5-9 KHz. Booting Linux, running libc init, and warming up before reaching the actual code you care about can take hours. Meanwhile, QEMU runs the same program **functionally** at MHz speed, but it has no notion of pipeline cycles. 8 9 The hybrid simulator combines them: run the boring parts in QEMU, switch to vsim for the small region of interest (ROI) you actually want to measure, and (optionally) hand back to QEMU to finish. 10 11 ## High-Level Concept 12 13 Three big ideas make this possible: 14 15 1. **Memory is shared, not copied.** Both simulators `mmap` the same backing file (`/dev/shm/...`) as their RAM. When QEMU writes a byte at address `0x80001000`, vsim sees it instantly, and vice versa. RAM size becomes irrelevant - no transfer cost. 16 17 2. **CPU state is small enough to copy.** The architectural registers (32 GPRs + 32 FPRs + a few CSRs) are only a few KB. We define a packed C struct (`struct hybrid_state_v1` in `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`) that both sides agree on. At a handoff, one side writes the struct to a file; the other side reads it. 18 19 3. **Microarchitectural state is thrown away.** Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer - none of it transfers. We accept cold caches at handoff and rely on a "warm-up window" before recording any measurements. 20 21 The trigger for a handoff is a **magic CSR write**: `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` means "QEMU, hand off to vsim", and `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` means "vsim, hand back to QEMU". The CSR address `0x7C0` is in the user-defined range, so it does not collide with anything real. 22 23 The write-back path on the vsim side reuses the existing **JTAG Debug Module** that the RTL already implements. Vsim drives the DM in-process (no socket, no OpenOCD) to halt the core, write all 32 GPRs, set the PC, and resume. The QEMU side uses a TCG plugin that drains state through the plugin API and exits cleanly. Hand-back from vsim to QEMU spawns a fresh QEMU process and restores state via QEMU's `gdbstub`. 24 25 ## Architecture Flowchart 26 27 ```mermaid 28 flowchart LR 29 subgraph DISK["Shared State (on disk / shm)"] 30 SHM[("/dev/shm RAM
(mmap shared)")] 31 STATE_Q["state_qemu.bin
(packed struct)"] 32 STATE_V["state_vsim.bin
(packed struct)"] 33 end 34 35 subgraph QEMU1["QEMU 1 (functional, MHz)"] 36 Q1_TCG["TCG instruction loop"] 37 Q1_PLUGIN["hybrid_handoff.so
watches for csrwi 0x7C0,0"] 38 Q1_TCG --> Q1_PLUGIN 39 end 40 41 subgraph VSIM["vsim (cycle-accurate, KHz)"] 42 V_PIPE["RTL pipeline
(Verilator + SystemC)"] 43 V_DM["JTAG DM driver
(in-process)"] 44 V_TRIG["csr_trigger
watches retire of csrwi 0x7C0,1"] 45 V_DM -.resumes regs.-> V_PIPE 46 V_PIPE -.retire signals.-> V_TRIG 47 end 48 49 subgraph QEMU2["QEMU 2 (functional, MHz)"] 50 Q2_GDB["gdbstub -gdb -S"] 51 Q2_TCG["TCG instruction loop"] 52 Q2_GDB -.restores regs.-> Q2_TCG 53 end 54 55 Q1_PLUGIN -- "drain GPR/FPR/CSR" --> STATE_Q 56 Q1_PLUGIN -- "qemu_plugin_request_exit" --> VSIM 57 STATE_Q -- "--resume-from-qemu" --> V_DM 58 59 V_TRIG -- "drain via DM" --> STATE_V 60 V_TRIG -- "exit code 200" --> QEMU2 61 STATE_V -- "gdb 'G' packet" --> Q2_GDB 62 63 Q1_TCG <-->|"every load/store"| SHM 64 V_PIPE <-->|"every load/store"| SHM 65 Q2_TCG <-->|"every load/store"| SHM 66 ``` 67 68 ## Walk-Through: `rt_shared_mem` E2E Test 69 70 This is the canonical hybrid-sim demo: it exercises **both** register transfer (via the state file) and memory transfer (via shared mmap) across all three simulators. Each phase mutates memory that the next phase observes. 71 72 ### The test program (`hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_shared_mem.S`) 73 74 ```asm 75 _start: 76 // Phase A (runs in QEMU 1): fill 4 KB at 0x80010000 with 0xA5 bytes. 77 li t0, 0x80010000 78 li t3, 0xA5A5A5A5A5A5A5A5 79 li t1, 512 80 1: sd t3, 0(t0) 81 addi t0, t0, 8 82 addi t1, t1, -1 83 bnez t1, 1b 84 85 csrwi 0x7C0, 0 // QEMU drains regs+PC -> state file, exits 86 87 // Phase B (runs in vsim): verify the 0xA5 pattern is still there, 88 // then XOR the page in place with 0x5A. After this loop every byte 89 // is 0xA5 ^ 0x5A = 0xFF. 90 li t0, 0x80010000 91 li t3, 0xA5A5A5A5A5A5A5A5 92 li t4, 0x5A5A5A5A5A5A5A5A 93 li t1, 512 94 verify_a: 95 ld t2, 0(t0) 96 bne t2, t3, fail_b // exit 50 if QEMU's writes did not survive 97 addi t0, t0, 8 98 addi t1, t1, -1 99 bnez t1, verify_a 100 li t0, 0x80010000 101 li t1, 512 102 xor_loop: 103 ld t2, 0(t0) 104 xor t2, t2, t4 105 sd t2, 0(t0) 106 addi t0, t0, 8 107 addi t1, t1, -1 108 bnez t1, xor_loop 109 110 csrwi 0x7C0, 1 // vsim drains regs+PC -> state file, exits 200 111 112 phase_c: // (runs in QEMU 2): verify all 4 KB is 0xFF. 113 li t0, 0x80010000 114 li t5, -1 115 li t1, 512 116 verify_c: 117 ld t2, 0(t0) 118 bne t2, t5, fail_c // exit 51 if vsim's writes did not survive 119 addi t0, t0, 8 120 addi t1, t1, -1 121 bnez t1, verify_c 122 li a2, 0 123 SEMIHOST_EXIT a2 // PASS: exit 0 124 ``` 125 126 Three phases, with **two kinds of state** crossing each handoff boundary: 127 128 | Boundary | Register state (via state file) | Memory state (via shared mmap) | 129 |---|---|---| 130 | QEMU 1 -> vsim | PC must point at the instruction after `csrwi 0x7C0, 0`. All 32 GPRs are copied through the packed struct. | The 4 KB of `0xA5` bytes Phase A wrote is already on disk in `/dev/shm/vsim-rt-shared.*`. vsim sees it instantly via mmap. | 131 | vsim -> QEMU 2 | PC must point at `phase_c`. GPRs copied back through a second state file. | The page is now `0xFF` because vsim XOR'd it. QEMU 2's mmap of the same file sees it byte-for-byte. | 132 133 If `bne t2, t3, fail_b` ever fires, QEMU's memory writes did not survive into vsim. If `bne t2, t5, fail_c` fires, vsim's writes did not survive into QEMU 2. The test only exits 0 when **both** transfer paths work end to end. 134 135 ### How register state moves: the state file 136 137 ``` 138 QEMU 1 vsim 139 hybrid_handoff.so state_qemu.bin qemu_resume.hpp 140 -------------------- ------------------ -------------------- 141 on csrwi 0x7C0, 0 ---> struct hybrid_state_v1 halt CPU via JTAG DM 142 read x1..x31 via write { magic = 'AHYB', for each reg: write 143 qemu_plugin_ atomic version = 1, via DM Abstract Cmd 144 read_register() with pc = , issue 'dret' -> resume 146 qemu_plugin_ gpr[32] = {...}, at the right PC 147 request_exit() csr = {...}, ... } 148 ``` 149 150 The same packed struct (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`, `struct hybrid_state_v1`) is the wire format on **both** sides. vsim drains symmetrically when it hits `csrwi 0x7C0, 1`, and the spawned QEMU 2 picks up the regs through its `gdbstub` `G` packet. 151 152 ### How memory state moves: shared mmap 153 154 ``` 155 host shell QEMU 1 vsim QEMU 2 156 truncate -s 128M 157 /dev/shm/foo 158 | 159 +-- mmap'd at 0x80000000 in all three guest address spaces ----+ 160 | | | | 161 v v v v 162 one 128 MB sd 0xA5... ld / xor / sd ld 0xFF... 163 backing file at GPA 0x80010000 at GPA 0x80010000 at GPA 0x80010000 164 -> file off 0x10000 -> same file off -> same file off 165 ``` 166 167 Both guests are launched with the same backing file: 168 - QEMU 1: `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=/dev/shm/foo -machine virt,memory-backend=mem` 169 - vsim: `--shared-mem-path /dev/shm/foo --shared-mem-size 128M` 170 - QEMU 2: spawned by vsim with the same `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,...` args. 171 172 A guest physical address `0x80010000` maps to file offset `0x10000` in every process. Loads and stores in any simulator hit the same OS page cache - no copying, no serialization, no synchronization needed beyond the handoff fence (each simulator has fully exited before the next starts). 173 174 ### The driver (`hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh`) 175 176 ```mermaid 177 sequenceDiagram 178 participant SH as rt_shared_mem.sh 179 participant FS as /dev/shm/foo
(128 MB mmap) 180 participant Q1 as QEMU 1 + plugin 181 participant VS as vsim 182 participant Q2 as QEMU 2 (spawned by vsim) 183 184 SH->>FS: truncate -s 128M (allocate empty backing file) 185 SH->>Q1: launch with -object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=foo
+ -plugin hybrid_handoff.so,outfile=state_qemu.bin 186 187 Note over Q1,FS: Phase A: writes 4 KB of 0xA5 at GPA 0x80010000 188 Q1->>FS: mmap stores -> file offset 0x10000 189 190 Q1->>Q1: hits csrwi 0x7C0, 0 191 Q1->>SH: writes state_qemu.bin (regs + PC), exits 192 193 SH->>VS: launch with --resume-from-qemu state_qemu.bin
--shared-mem-path foo --qemu-binary qemu... 194 VS->>VS: JTAG DM restores 32 GPRs and PC from state_qemu.bin 195 VS->>FS: ld at 0x80010000 -> reads 0xA5 (QEMU's writes survived!) 196 Note over VS,FS: Phase B: XOR loop -> page becomes 0xFF 197 VS->>FS: sd at 0x80010000 -> file now contains 0xFF 198 199 VS->>VS: hits csrwi 0x7C0, 1, drains regs+PC to state_vsim.bin 200 VS->>Q2: fork+exec qemu -gdb tcp:... -S -semihosting
-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=foo 201 VS->>Q2: gdb 'G' packet with regs from state_vsim.bin, then 'c' 202 Q2->>FS: ld at 0x80010000 -> reads 0xFF (vsim's writes survived!) 203 Note over Q2: Phase C verify loop passes 204 Q2->>VS: SEMIHOST_EXIT 0 205 VS->>SH: process exits with code 0 206 SH->>SH: assert exit code == 0 -> PASS 207 ``` 208 209 ### What each step proves 210 211 | Step | What it tests | 212 |---|---| 213 | Phase A writes 0xA5 to mmap | QEMU's RAM backend is the shared file (not a private anonymous mapping). | 214 | QEMU 1 produces `state_qemu.bin` | The TCG plugin recognizes `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` and drains all 32 GPRs + PC + CSRs through the plugin API. | 215 | Phase B `verify_a` loop passes | **Memory written in QEMU survives into vsim.** Shared mmap works as advertised. | 216 | vsim runs the XOR loop | Resumed register state was correct - in particular `t0`, `t1`, `t3`, `t4` are reinitialized by Phase B but the **PC** must have been restored to point at Phase B's first instruction. | 217 | Phase C `verify_c` loop passes | **Memory written in vsim survives into QEMU 2.** Both transfer paths work. | 218 | Final exit code 0 | Full round-trip succeeded. Exit 50 means QEMU->vsim memory broke; exit 51 means vsim->QEMU memory broke. | 219 220 If any link breaks, the distinct exit codes tell you exactly which transfer path failed. 221 222 ## Why the Asymmetry? File for QEMU->vsim, gdbstub for vsim->QEMU 223 224 A natural question on first reading: vsim reads register state from a file, but QEMU 2 receives it through gdbstub. Why not use a file in both directions? 225 226 **Short answer**: QEMU has no built-in "load architectural state from a file before fetch starts" hook. gdbstub is exactly that hook, already shipping in mainline QEMU. 227 228 The whole design rests on one rule (`hybrid_plan.md` Section 2.4): **no QEMU patches, no fork**. Mainline QEMU 9.2.0 is used as-is. That ships exactly two extension points relevant to state injection: 229 230 | Extension point | What it can do | 231 |---|---| 232 | TCG plugin API (`qemu_plugin_*`) | **Read** registers and memory while QEMU runs. `qemu_plugin_read_register()` exists and is reliable for all reg classes. There is no clean way to set all 32 GPRs + PC + CSRs *before the very first TB executes* - plugins are designed for instrumentation, not state injection at startup. | 233 | gdbstub (`-gdb tcp::PORT -S`) | Stops QEMU before any guest instruction runs and opens a port speaking the standard RSP protocol. Whoever connects can write all registers, then resume. This is exactly the "set state, then start" primitive we need. | 234 235 So each direction picks the only working mechanism mainline QEMU offers: 236 237 ``` 238 QEMU 1 -> vsim: plugin reads regs (read_register works) 239 plugin writes file at exit 240 vsim reads file 241 vsim has the JTAG DM already, drives it in-process to WRITE 242 regs into RTL via Abstract Commands. 243 244 vsim -> QEMU 2: vsim drains regs via JTAG DM into a state file. 245 spawn `qemu -gdb tcp::PORT -S` (start halted). 246 vsim connects, sends 'G' packet (regs from the file), 'c'. 247 QEMU does not parse the file - vsim translates file -> RSP packet. 248 ``` 249 250 Why not "QEMU 2 reads the file itself"? 251 252 1. **No CLI flag exists.** `qemu-system-riscv64` has no `--load-state-from ` for an arbitrary register struct. Adding one means patching QEMU - ruled out. 253 2. **Plugins can't do it either.** A plugin's earliest hook (`vcpu_init`, first TB callback) runs *after* the reset vector executes some instructions. Setting the PC from a plugin at that point fights QEMU's fetch loop. 254 3. **gdbstub is purpose-built for this.** `-S` (start halted) plus the RSP `G` packet are the canonical way to externally seed QEMU state. Mature, covers every reg class the gdb XML exposes, works for every machine type. 255 4. **Decouples the wire format.** vsim translates `struct hybrid_state_v1` into RSP packets. QEMU 2 only sees a standard gdb client. No coupling between QEMU and the project's struct definition. 256 257 Memory does not face this problem because QEMU **does** have a file-backed memory primitive (`-object memory-backend-file,share=on`), so the same mmap trick works on both QEMU instances. Registers don't have an equivalent "memory-backend-file for regs" - gdbstub is the closest thing, and it happens to be enough. 258 259 ### What is the "RSP `G` packet"? 260 261 **RSP** = Remote Serial Protocol, the text-based wire protocol gdb uses to talk to a remote target (gdbserver, QEMU's gdbstub, OpenOCD, J-Link, etc.). Every packet has the form `$#`. 262 263 **The `G` packet** is one specific RSP command: *"write all CPU registers at once."* Its payload is a single hex string concatenating every register's bytes in the exact order the target's gdb XML description declares them. 264 265 Companion packets in the same family: 266 267 | Packet | Meaning | 268 |---|---| 269 | `g` (lowercase) | read all registers - target replies with the hex blob | 270 | `G` (uppercase) | write all registers - sender provides the hex blob | 271 | `p N` / `P N=...` | read / write a single register by index | 272 | `m addr,len` / `M addr,len:...` | read / write memory | 273 | `c` | continue execution | 274 | `s` | single-step | 275 276 For RV64 with FP and CSRs, the `G` payload looks like (hex, little-endian per register): 277 278 ``` 279 $G.........#xx 280 \____________ regs in gdb-XML order ____________/ 281 ``` 282 283 Concretely, what vsim does in the handback (see `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/gdbstub_session.hpp` and `gdbstub_client.hpp`): 284 285 ``` 286 1. spawn qemu-system-riscv64 -gdb tcp::PORT -S -kernel elf 287 (QEMU opens PORT, halts before any insn fetch) 288 289 2. connect TCP, do gdb handshake (qSupported, qXfer:features:read, ...) 290 291 3. build the hex string from struct hybrid_state_v1: 292 concat hex(state.gpr[0..31]) + hex(state.pc) + hex(state.fpr[...]) + ... 293 294 4. send $G# -> QEMU replies "OK" 295 296 5. send $c# -> QEMU starts executing at state.pc 297 ``` 298 299 That's the entire protocol exchange. No QEMU code changes; no custom file format on the QEMU side; just standard gdb RSP. Reference: GDB Remote Serial Protocol, sections "Packets" and "Register Packet Format" in the gdb manual. 300 301 ## Where to Look Next 302 303 - `include/hybrid/state_abi.h` - the wire contract; both QEMU plugin and vsim include this exact header. 304 - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` - the QEMU side. Look at `resolve_handles` and the entry-trigger callback. 305 - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/` - the vsim side: `qemu_resume.hpp`, `state_drain.hpp`, `csr_trigger.hpp`, `qemu_handback.hpp`. 306 - `hybrid_vsim/hybrid_plan.md` - the original design document, with full rationale for every decision. 307 - `run_e2e.sh` - run all e2e tests locally without going through CMake/CTest. 308 309 ## Mental Model in One Sentence 310 311 > Two simulators agree on a memory map and a register struct, take turns running the same program, and use a magic CSR write as the baton-pass. 312

Each phase mutates memory that the next phase observes. ### The test program (`hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_shared_mem.S`) ```asm _start: // Phase A (runs in QEMU 1): fill 4 KB at 0x80010000 with 0xA5 bytes. li t0, 0x80010000 li t3, 0xA5A5A5A5A5A5A5A5 li t1, 512 1: sd t3, 0(t0) addi t0, t0, 8 addi t1, t1, -1 bnez t1, 1b csrwi 0x7C0, 0 // QEMU drains regs+PC -> state file, exits // Phase B (runs in vsim): verify the 0xA5 pattern is still there, // then XOR the page in place with 0x5A. After this loop every byte // is 0xA5 ^ 0x5A = 0xFF. li t0, 0x80010000 li t3, 0xA5A5A5A5A5A5A5A5 li t4, 0x5A5A5A5A5A5A5A5A li t1, 512 verify_a: ld t2, 0(t0) bne t2, t3, fail_b // exit 50 if QEMU's writes did not survive addi t0, t0, 8 addi t1, t1, -1 bnez t1, verify_a li t0, 0x80010000 li t1, 512 xor_loop: ld t2, 0(t0) xor t2, t2, t4 sd t2, 0(t0) addi t0, t0, 8 addi t1, t1, -1 bnez t1, xor_loop csrwi 0x7C0, 1 // vsim drains regs+PC -> state file, exits 200 phase_c: // (runs in QEMU 2): verify all 4 KB is 0xFF. li t0, 0x80010000 li t5, -1 li t1, 512 verify_c: ld t2, 0(t0) bne t2, t5, fail_c // exit 51 if vsim's writes did not survive addi t0, t0, 8 addi t1, t1, -1 bnez t1, verify_c li a2, 0 SEMIHOST_EXIT a2 // PASS: exit 0 ``` Three phases, with **two kinds of state** crossing each handoff boundary: | Boundary | Register state (via state file) | Memory state (via shared mmap) | |---|---|---| | QEMU 1 -> vsim | PC must point at the instruction after `csrwi 0x7C0, 0`. All 32 GPRs are copied through the packed struct. | The 4 KB of `0xA5` bytes Phase A wrote is already on disk in `/dev/shm/vsim-rt-shared.*`. vsim sees it instantly via mmap. | | vsim -> QEMU 2 | PC must point at `phase_c`. GPRs copied back through a second state file. | The page is now `0xFF` because vsim XOR'd it. QEMU 2's mmap of the same file sees it byte-for-byte. | If `bne t2, t3, fail_b` ever fires, QEMU's memory writes did not survive into vsim. If `bne t2, t5, fail_c` fires, vsim's writes did not survive into QEMU 2. The test only exits 0 when **both** transfer paths work end to end. ### How register state moves: the state file ``` QEMU 1 vsim hybrid_handoff.so state_qemu.bin qemu_resume.hpp -------------------- ------------------ -------------------- on csrwi 0x7C0, 0 ---> struct hybrid_state_v1 halt CPU via JTAG DM read x1..x31 via write { magic = 'AHYB', for each reg: write qemu_plugin_ atomic version = 1, via DM Abstract Cmd read_register() with pc = , issue 'dret' -> resume qemu_plugin_ gpr[32] = {...}, at the right PC request_exit() csr = {...}, ... } ``` The same packed struct (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`, `struct hybrid_state_v1`) is the wire format on **both** sides. vsim drains symmetrically when it hits `csrwi 0x7C0, 1`, and the spawned QEMU 2 picks up the regs through its `gdbstub` `G` packet. ### How memory state moves: shared mmap ``` host shell QEMU 1 vsim QEMU 2 truncate -s 128M /dev/shm/foo | +-- mmap'd at 0x80000000 in all three guest address spaces ----+ | | | | v v v v one 128 MB sd 0xA5... ld / xor / sd ld 0xFF... backing file at GPA 0x80010000 at GPA 0x80010000 at GPA 0x80010000 -> file off 0x10000 -> same file off -> same file off ``` Both guests are launched with the same backing file: - QEMU 1: `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=/dev/shm/foo -machine virt,memory-backend=mem` - vsim: `--shared-mem-path /dev/shm/foo --shared-mem-size 128M` - QEMU 2: spawned by vsim with the same `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,...` args. A guest physical address `0x80010000` maps to file offset `0x10000` in every process. Loads and stores in any simulator hit the same OS page cache - no copying, no serialization, no synchronization needed beyond the handoff fence (each simulator has fully exited before the next starts). ### The driver (`hybrid_vsim/tests/hybrid/rt_shared_mem.sh`) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant SH as rt_shared_mem.sh participant FS as /dev/shm/foo
(128 MB mmap) participant Q1 as QEMU 1 + plugin participant VS as vsim participant Q2 as QEMU 2 (spawned by vsim) SH->>FS: truncate -s 128M (allocate empty backing file) SH->>Q1: launch with -object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=foo
+ -plugin hybrid_handoff.so,outfile=state_qemu.bin Note over Q1,FS: Phase A: writes 4 KB of 0xA5 at GPA 0x80010000 Q1->>FS: mmap stores -> file offset 0x10000 Q1->>Q1: hits csrwi 0x7C0, 0 Q1->>SH: writes state_qemu.bin (regs + PC), exits SH->>VS: launch with --resume-from-qemu state_qemu.bin
--shared-mem-path foo --qemu-binary qemu... VS->>VS: JTAG DM restores 32 GPRs and PC from state_qemu.bin VS->>FS: ld at 0x80010000 -> reads 0xA5 (QEMU's writes survived!) Note over VS,FS: Phase B: XOR loop -> page becomes 0xFF VS->>FS: sd at 0x80010000 -> file now contains 0xFF VS->>VS: hits csrwi 0x7C0, 1, drains regs+PC to state_vsim.bin VS->>Q2: fork+exec qemu -gdb tcp:... -S -semihosting
-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=foo VS->>Q2: gdb 'G' packet with regs from state_vsim.bin, then 'c' Q2->>FS: ld at 0x80010000 -> reads 0xFF (vsim's writes survived!) Note over Q2: Phase C verify loop passes Q2->>VS: SEMIHOST_EXIT 0 VS->>SH: process exits with code 0 SH->>SH: assert exit code == 0 -> PASS ``` ### What each step proves | Step | What it tests | |---|---| | Phase A writes 0xA5 to mmap | QEMU's RAM backend is the shared file (not a private anonymous mapping). | | QEMU 1 produces `state_qemu.bin` | The TCG plugin recognizes `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` and drains all 32 GPRs + PC + CSRs through the plugin API. | | Phase B `verify_a` loop passes | **Memory written in QEMU survives into vsim.** Shared mmap works as advertised. | | vsim runs the XOR loop | Resumed register state was correct - in particular `t0`, `t1`, `t3`, `t4` are reinitialized by Phase B but the **PC** must have been restored to point at Phase B's first instruction. | | Phase C `verify_c` loop passes | **Memory written in vsim survives into QEMU 2.** Both transfer paths work. | | Final exit code 0 | Full round-trip succeeded. Exit 50 means QEMU->vsim memory broke; exit 51 means vsim->QEMU memory broke. | If any link breaks, the distinct exit codes tell you exactly which transfer path failed. ## Why the Asymmetry? File for QEMU->vsim, gdbstub for vsim->QEMU A natural question on first reading: vsim reads register state from a file, but QEMU 2 receives it through gdbstub. Why not use a file in both directions? **Short answer**: QEMU has no built-in "load architectural state from a file before fetch starts" hook. gdbstub is exactly that hook, already shipping in mainline QEMU. The whole design rests on one rule (`hybrid_plan.md` Section 2.4): **no QEMU patches, no fork**. Mainline QEMU 9.2.0 is used as-is. That ships exactly two extension points relevant to state injection: | Extension point | What it can do | |---|---| | TCG plugin API (`qemu_plugin_*`) | **Read** registers and memory while QEMU runs. `qemu_plugin_read_register()` exists and is reliable for all reg classes. There is no clean way to set all 32 GPRs + PC + CSRs *before the very first TB executes* - plugins are designed for instrumentation, not state injection at startup. | | gdbstub (`-gdb tcp::PORT -S`) | Stops QEMU before any guest instruction runs and opens a port speaking the standard RSP protocol. Whoever connects can write all registers, then resume. This is exactly the "set state, then start" primitive we need. | So each direction picks the only working mechanism mainline QEMU offers: ``` QEMU 1 -> vsim: plugin reads regs (read_register works) plugin writes file at exit vsim reads file vsim has the JTAG DM already, drives it in-process to WRITE regs into RTL via Abstract Commands. vsim -> QEMU 2: vsim drains regs via JTAG DM into a state file. spawn `qemu -gdb tcp::PORT -S` (start halted). vsim connects, sends 'G' packet (regs from the file), 'c'. QEMU does not parse the file - vsim translates file -> RSP packet. ``` Why not "QEMU 2 reads the file itself"? 1. **No CLI flag exists.** `qemu-system-riscv64` has no `--load-state-from ` for an arbitrary register struct. Adding one means patching QEMU - ruled out. 2. **Plugins can't do it either.** A plugin's earliest hook (`vcpu_init`, first TB callback) runs *after* the reset vector executes some instructions. Setting the PC from a plugin at that point fights QEMU's fetch loop. 3. **gdbstub is purpose-built for this.** `-S` (start halted) plus the RSP `G` packet are the canonical way to externally seed QEMU state. Mature, covers every reg class the gdb XML exposes, works for every machine type. 4. **Decouples the wire format.** vsim translates `struct hybrid_state_v1` into RSP packets. QEMU 2 only sees a standard gdb client. No coupling between QEMU and the project's struct definition. Memory does not face this problem because QEMU **does** have a file-backed memory primitive (`-object memory-backend-file,share=on`), so the same mmap trick works on both QEMU instances. Registers don't have an equivalent "memory-backend-file for regs" - gdbstub is the closest thing, and it happens to be enough. ### What is the "RSP `G` packet"? **RSP** = Remote Serial Protocol, the text-based wire protocol gdb uses to talk to a remote target (gdbserver, QEMU's gdbstub, OpenOCD, J-Link, etc.). Every packet has the form `$#`. **The `G` packet** is one specific RSP command: *"write all CPU registers at once."* Its payload is a single hex string concatenating every register's bytes in the exact order the target's gdb XML description declares them. Companion packets in the same family: | Packet | Meaning | |---|---| | `g` (lowercase) | read all registers - target replies with the hex blob | | `G` (uppercase) | write all registers - sender provides the hex blob | | `p N` / `P N=...` | read / write a single register by index | | `m addr,len` / `M addr,len:...` | read / write memory | | `c` | continue execution | | `s` | single-step | For RV64 with FP and CSRs, the `G` payload looks like (hex, little-endian per register): ``` $G.........#xx \____________ regs in gdb-XML order ____________/ ``` Concretely, what vsim does in the handback (see `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/gdbstub_session.hpp` and `gdbstub_client.hpp`): ``` 1. spawn qemu-system-riscv64 -gdb tcp::PORT -S -kernel elf (QEMU opens PORT, halts before any insn fetch) 2. connect TCP, do gdb handshake (qSupported, qXfer:features:read, ...) 3. build the hex string from struct hybrid_state_v1: concat hex(state.gpr[0..31]) + hex(state.pc) + hex(state.fpr[...]) + ... 4. send $G# -> QEMU replies "OK" 5. send $c# -> QEMU starts executing at state.pc ``` That's the entire protocol exchange. No QEMU code changes; no custom file format on the QEMU side; just standard gdb RSP. Reference: GDB Remote Serial Protocol, sections "Packets" and "Register Packet Format" in the gdb manual. ## Where to Look Next - `include/hybrid/state_abi.h` - the wire contract; both QEMU plugin and vsim include this exact header. - `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` - the QEMU side. Look at `resolve_handles` and the entry-trigger callback. - `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/` - the vsim side: `qemu_resume.hpp`, `state_drain.hpp`, `csr_trigger.hpp`, `qemu_handback.hpp`. - `hybrid_vsim/hybrid_plan.md` - the original design document, with full rationale for every decision. - `run_e2e.sh` - run all e2e tests locally without going through CMake/CTest. ## Mental Model in One Sentence > Two simulators agree on a memory map and a register struct, take turns running the same program, and use a magic CSR write as the baton-pass.

1	/* Tier-2 C-level round-trip: RVV register survival across the handoff.
2	 *
3	 *   QEMU1: seed an int32 pattern in shared memory (no V instructions; the
4	 *          QEMU plugin's per-insn callback misreports the drained PC when
5	 *          the immediately-preceding code uses V).
6	 *   vsim:  vle32.v v8..v15 from the seed; vadd.vi v9 += 100; vse8.v
7	 *          v0..v31 to the V-scratch region.
8	 *   QEMU2: vle8.v v0..v31 from V-scratch (round-trip the regfile back into
9	 *          QEMU's V state), then vse32.v v8..v15 + verify; v8/v10..v15
10	 *          unchanged from seed, v9 == seed + 100.
11	 *
12	 * V-state transport
13	 *   The vsim hart's V regfile is not exposed via Debug Module Abstract
14	 *   Commands (the spec leaves V to optional Program Buffer / DPI). As a
15	 *   stop-gap we use a fixed scratch region inside the shared mmap (V_SCRATCH
16	 *   below, matching hybrid::kVScratchGpa). vsim's StateDrain reads those
17	 *   bytes into hybrid_state_v1::vreg[] so qemu_handback can ship them to
18	 *   QEMU 2 via existing gdbstub P packets. ResumeDriver does the inverse on
19	 *   the way in, but this fixture's QEMU1 phase doesn't use V so the
20	 *   inbound V state is all zeros -- vsim re-seeds v0..v31 itself by
21	 *   loading from the SEED region.
22	 *
23	 * Memory layout:
24	 *   SEED at 0x80100000        - input pattern (16 int32, 64 bytes)
25	 *   PHASE_C_OUT at 0x80102000 - QEMU2 verify scratch (8 regs * 64 = 512 B)
26	 *   V_SCRATCH at 0x80FF0000   - matches hybrid::kVScratchGpa (32 V regs *
27	 *                                64 bytes = 2048 B)
28	 *   stack at 0x80700000       - rt_c.ld places _stack here
29	 *
30	 * VLEN contract: VLEN=512 (vlenb=64). QEMU launched with
31	 * -cpu andes-ax45mpv,vlen=512; vsim built from
32	 * data/configs/ax45mpv_premium.cfg with NDS_VLEN 512.
33	 */
34	#include "runtime/rt_c_helpers.h"
35	
36	#include <stdint.h>
37	
38	#define VL_E32 16  /* VLEN/32 = 512/32 = 16 int32 elements per V reg at LMUL=1 */
39	#define VLENB  64  /* bytes per V reg */
40	#define NREGS  8   /* test v8..v15 */
41	
42	static int32_t* const SEED        = (int32_t*) 0x80100000UL;
43	static int32_t* const PHASE_C_OUT = (int32_t*) 0x80102000UL;
44	
45	#define V_SCRATCH 0x80FF0000UL
46	
47	static void seed_pattern(int32_t* dst, int n)
48	{
49	  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
50	    dst[i] = (int32_t) ((i * 17) ^ 0xA5A5);
51	}
52	
53	int main(void)
54	{
55	  /* Phase 1 (QEMU1): seed memory only. No V instructions here -- the
56	   * plugin's gdbstub-pc readback misreports the drain PC when V is in
57	   * flight, which would resume vsim at the wrong instruction. */
58	  seed_pattern(SEED, VL_E32);

60	  rt_phase_qemu1_drain();

63	   * v0..v31 regfile to V_SCRATCH so vsim's drain can read them out for

66	    /* Reload v8..v15 from SEED via 32-bit unit-stride loads. */
67	    "li     a4, 16\n"
68	    "vsetvli zero, a4, e32, m1, ta, ma\n"
69	    "mv     t0, %0\n"
70	    "vle32.v v8,  (t0)\n"
71	    "vle32.v v9,  (t0)\n"
72	    "vle32.v v10, (t0)\n"
73	    "vle32.v v11, (t0)\n"
74	    "vle32.v v12, (t0)\n"
75	    "vle32.v v13, (t0)\n"
76	    "vle32.v v14, (t0)\n"
77	    "vle32.v v15, (t0)\n"

80	    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
81	    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
82	    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
83	    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
84	    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
85	    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"

87	    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"

90	     * exactly vlenb bytes per reg, regardless of element semantics). */
91	    "li     a4, 64\n"
92	    "vsetvli zero, a4, e8, m1, ta, ma\n"
93	    "li     t0, 0x80FF0000\n"

95	    "vse8.v v1,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
96	    "vse8.v v2,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
97	    "vse8.v v3,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"

132	   * already populated v0..v31 in QEMU's V state from state.vreg[]. Run

139	   * read-only zero and vsetvli would trap. */

141	    "li     a4, 16\n"
142	    "vsetvli zero, a4, e32, m1, ta, ma\n"
143	    "addi t0, %0, 0\n"

/* Tier-2 C-level round-trip: RVV register survival across the handoff.
 *
 *   QEMU1: seed an int32 pattern in shared memory (no V instructions; the
 *          QEMU plugin's per-insn callback misreports the drained PC when
 *          the immediately-preceding code uses V).
 *   vsim:  vle32.v v8..v15 from the seed; vadd.vi v9 += 100; vse8.v
 *          v0..v31 to the V-scratch region.
 *   QEMU2: vle8.v v0..v31 from V-scratch (round-trip the regfile back into
 *          QEMU's V state), then vse32.v v8..v15 + verify; v8/v10..v15
 *          unchanged from seed, v9 == seed + 100.
 *
 * V-state transport
 *   The vsim hart's V regfile is not exposed via Debug Module Abstract
 *   Commands (the spec leaves V to optional Program Buffer / DPI). As a
 *   stop-gap we use a fixed scratch region inside the shared mmap (V_SCRATCH
 *   below, matching hybrid::kVScratchGpa). vsim's StateDrain reads those
 *   bytes into hybrid_state_v1::vreg[] so qemu_handback can ship them to
 *   QEMU 2 via existing gdbstub P packets. ResumeDriver does the inverse on
 *   the way in, but this fixture's QEMU1 phase doesn't use V so the
 *   inbound V state is all zeros -- vsim re-seeds v0..v31 itself by
 *   loading from the SEED region.
 *
 * Memory layout:
 *   SEED at 0x80100000        - input pattern (16 int32, 64 bytes)
 *   PHASE_C_OUT at 0x80102000 - QEMU2 verify scratch (8 regs * 64 = 512 B)
 *   V_SCRATCH at 0x80FF0000   - matches hybrid::kVScratchGpa (32 V regs *
 *                                64 bytes = 2048 B)
 *   stack at 0x80700000       - rt_c.ld places _stack here
 *
 * VLEN contract: VLEN=512 (vlenb=64). QEMU launched with
 * -cpu andes-ax45mpv,vlen=512; vsim built from
 * data/configs/ax45mpv_premium.cfg with NDS_VLEN 512.
 */
#include "runtime/rt_c_helpers.h"

#include <stdint.h>

#define VL_E32 16  /* VLEN/32 = 512/32 = 16 int32 elements per V reg at LMUL=1 */
#define VLENB  64  /* bytes per V reg */
#define NREGS  8   /* test v8..v15 */

static int32_t* const SEED        = (int32_t*) 0x80100000UL;
static int32_t* const PHASE_C_OUT = (int32_t*) 0x80102000UL;

#define V_SCRATCH 0x80FF0000UL

static void seed_pattern(int32_t* dst, int n)
{
  for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    dst[i] = (int32_t) ((i * 17) ^ 0xA5A5);
}

int main(void)
{
  /* Phase 1 (QEMU1): seed memory only. No V instructions here -- the
   * plugin's gdbstub-pc readback misreports the drain PC when V is in
   * flight, which would resume vsim at the wrong instruction. */
  seed_pattern(SEED, VL_E32);

  rt_phase_qemu1_drain();

  /* Phase 2 (vsim): load v8..v15 from SEED, mutate v9, then save the full
   * v0..v31 regfile to V_SCRATCH so vsim's drain can read them out for
   * the QEMU 2 hop. mstatus.VS is restored by ResumeDriver (Tier-A). */
  asm volatile(
    /* Reload v8..v15 from SEED via 32-bit unit-stride loads. */
    "li     a4, 16\n"
    "vsetvli zero, a4, e32, m1, ta, ma\n"
    "mv     t0, %0\n"
    "vle32.v v8,  (t0)\n"
    "vle32.v v9,  (t0)\n"
    "vle32.v v10, (t0)\n"
    "vle32.v v11, (t0)\n"
    "vle32.v v12, (t0)\n"
    "vle32.v v13, (t0)\n"
    "vle32.v v14, (t0)\n"
    "vle32.v v15, (t0)\n"
    /* Mutate v9 by +100 (vadd.vi takes 5-bit signed imm; +10 ten times). */
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    "vadd.vi v9, v9, 10\n"
    /* Save v0..v31 to V_SCRATCH for vsim's drain (e8 stride to write
     * exactly vlenb bytes per reg, regardless of element semantics). */
    "li     a4, 64\n"
    "vsetvli zero, a4, e8, m1, ta, ma\n"
    "li     t0, 0x80FF0000\n"
    "vse8.v v0,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v1,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v2,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v3,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v4,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v5,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v6,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v7,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v8,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v9,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v10, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v11, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v12, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v13, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v14, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v15, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v16, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v17, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v18, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v19, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v20, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v21, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v22, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v23, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v24, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v25, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v26, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v27, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v28, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v29, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v30, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse8.v v31, (t0)\n"
    :: "r"(SEED)
    : "memory","t0","a4");

  rt_phase_vsim_drain();

  /* Phase 3 (QEMU2): the gdbstub P packets (sent by qemu_handback) have
   * already populated v0..v31 in QEMU's V state from state.vreg[]. Run
   * vse32.v v8..v15 to PHASE_C_OUT and verify the bytes match the seed
   * pattern (with v9 +100).
   *
   * mstatus.VS is restored by ResumeDriver so no csrs needed. QEMU 2
   * must be spawned with -cpu andes-ax45mpv,vlen=512 (see
   * qemu_handback.hpp::spawn_qemu) - the default rv64 CPU has VS as
   * read-only zero and vsetvli would trap. */
  asm volatile(
    "li     a4, 16\n"
    "vsetvli zero, a4, e32, m1, ta, ma\n"
    "addi t0, %0, 0\n"
    "vse32.v v8,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse32.v v9,  (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse32.v v10, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse32.v v11, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse32.v v12, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse32.v v13, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse32.v v14, (t0)\n addi t0, t0, 64\n"
    "vse32.v v15, (t0)\n"
    :: "r"(PHASE_C_OUT)
    : "memory","t0","a4");

  for (int reg = 0; reg < NREGS; reg++) {
    int32_t* row = PHASE_C_OUT + reg * VL_E32;
    int32_t bias = (reg == 1) ? 100 : 0;  /* v9 is reg index 1 in v8..v15 */
    for (int i = 0; i < VL_E32; i++) {
      int32_t expected = (int32_t) ((i * 17) ^ 0xA5A5) + bias;
      if (row[i] != expected) {
        rt_semihost_writes("rt_c_v_regs: FAIL\n");
        return 1;
      }
    }
  }
  rt_semihost_writes("rt_c_v_regs: PASS\n");
  return 0;
}

1	// V-register transport via shared scratch RAM. The vsim Debug Module
2	// abstract-command space does not allocate slots for v0..v31 (RISC-V Debug
3	// Spec leaves V to optional Program Buffer or implementation-specific
4	// extensions), and the AX45MPV CPU wrapper does not export a DPI probe for
5	// the V regfile. As a stop-gap that keeps Phase 5 (DPI / PB) deferred while
6	// still letting C-level RVV fixtures round-trip:
7	//
8	//   - On resume from QEMU 1, vsim copies state.vreg[] into a fixed scratch
9	//     region of guest RAM (kVScratchGpa). The fixture's vsim-phase prologue
10	//     issues `vle8.v vN, (t0)` against that region to repopulate v0..v31.
11	//   - Before the vsim->QEMU2 drain trigger, the fixture issues `vse8.v vN,
12	//     (t0)` to write the (possibly mutated) regfile back to the same region.
13	//     vsim's StateDrain side reads the scratch bytes into state.vreg[] so
14	//     qemu_handback can ship them to QEMU 2 via the existing gdbstub P
15	//     packets.
16	//
17	// The contract is fixture-aware: any program that wants V state preserved
18	// across the vsim boundary must perform the load/store at the boundaries.
19	// rt_c_v_regs.c demonstrates the pattern. Phase 5 will replace this with a
20	// transparent mechanism (PB or DPI) so benchmark binaries don't need to be
21	// modified.
22	//
23	// Pure logic: no SystemC, no DMI bus. Easy to unit-test.
24	
25	#pragma once
26	
27	#include "hybrid/state_abi.h"
28	
29	#include <cstddef>
30	#include <cstdint>
31	#include <cstring>
32	#include <span>
33	#include <stdexcept>
34	
35	namespace hybrid
36	{
37	
38	// Top of QEMU virt's default 128 MiB RAM (RAM_BASE 0x80000000 + 0x0FF0000
39	// = 0x80FF0000). Below the C-runtime stack at 0x80700000 only when the
40	// runtime grows beyond default; rt_c.ld places _stack at 0x80700000 so
41	// 0x80FF0000 sits well above it. Document the reservation in fixture
42	// comments so future memory-layout changes don't collide.
43	inline constexpr uint64_t kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000ull;
44	
45	// Total scratch bytes needed: 32 V regs × HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX. Sized to
46	// the maximum supported VLEN; the actual per-reg byte count is `vlenb`.
47	inline constexpr std::size_t kVScratchBytes =
48	  static_cast<std::size_t>(32) * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX;
49	
50	namespace detail
51	{
52	
53	// Translate scratch GPA into a byte offset inside a RAM span whose first
54	// byte sits at `ram_base`. Throws if the scratch region falls outside the
55	// supplied span (i.e. the caller passed a too-small RAM mapping).
56	inline auto scratch_offset(std::size_t ram_size, uint64_t ram_base)
57	  -> std::size_t
58	{
59	  if (kVScratchGpa < ram_base)
60	    throw std::runtime_error(
61	      "v_regfile_xfer: scratch GPA below RAM base");
62	  const auto off = static_cast<std::size_t>(kVScratchGpa - ram_base);
63	  if (off + kVScratchBytes > ram_size)
64	    throw std::runtime_error(
65	      "v_regfile_xfer: scratch region exceeds RAM span");
66	  return off;
67	}
68	
69	}  // namespace detail
70	
71	// Copy state.vreg[] into ram[scratch_offset .. scratch_offset+kVScratchBytes].
72	// Called just before vsim resumes the hart so the fixture can vle8.v from
73	// scratch and pick up QEMU 1's V values.
74	inline void stage_v_to_scratch(const hybrid_state_v1& state,
75	                               std::span<uint8_t> ram,
76	                               uint64_t ram_base)
77	{
78	  const auto off = detail::scratch_offset(ram.size(), ram_base);
79	  std::memcpy(ram.data() + off, state.vreg, kVScratchBytes);
80	}
81	
82	// Copy ram[scratch_offset .. +kVScratchBytes] into state.vreg[]. Called
83	// inside the vsim drain path after the fixture's prologue vse8.v has
84	// settled the (possibly mutated) regfile bytes into RAM.
85	inline void read_v_from_scratch(hybrid_state_v1& state,
86	                                std::span<const uint8_t> ram,
87	                                uint64_t ram_base)
88	{
89	  const auto off = detail::scratch_offset(ram.size(), ram_base);
90	  std::memcpy(state.vreg, ram.data() + off, kVScratchBytes);
91	}
92	
93	}  // namespace hybrid

# How the Hybrid Simulator Works A friendly tour for engineers new to this codebase. ## The Problem in One Paragraph A pure RTL simulator (`vsim`, built on Verilator + SystemC) is **cycle-accurate**: it tells you exactly how many cycles every instruction takes inside the CPU pipeline. The price is speed: it runs at ~5-9 KHz. Booting Linux, running libc init, and warming up before reaching the actual code you care about can take hours. Meanwhile, QEMU runs the same program **functionally** at MHz speed, but it has no notion of pipeline cycles. The hybrid simulator combines them: run the boring parts in QEMU, switch to vsim for the small region of interest (ROI) you actually want to measure, and (optionally) hand back to QEMU to finish. ## High-Level Concept Four big ideas make this possible: 1. **Memory is shared, not copied.** Both simulators `mmap` the same backing file (`/dev/shm/...`) as their RAM. When QEMU writes a byte at `0x80001000`, vsim sees it instantly, and vice versa. RAM size becomes irrelevant - no transfer cost. 2. **Architectural CPU state is small enough to copy through one packed struct.** GPRs, FPRs, M-mode CSRs, the V regfile (`v0..v31`), and V scalar CSRs (`vtype`, `vl`, `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`) are all described by `struct hybrid_state_v1` in `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`. That struct is the wire contract every component agrees on, written to a file at each handoff. 3. **The trigger is a magic CSR write.** `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` = "QEMU, hand off to vsim". `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` = "vsim, hand back to QEMU". CSR `0x7C0` lives in the user-defined range so it cannot collide with anything real. 4. **Microarchitectural state is thrown away.** Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer - none of it transfers. We accept cold caches at handoff and rely on a "warm-up window" before recording measurements. ## State Categories and Their Transport The wire struct is uniform, but each category travels through a different host primitive depending on what QEMU and the RTL Debug Module are willing to expose. | Category | Out of QEMU 1 | Into vsim | Out of vsim | Into QEMU 2 | |---------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------| | GPRs, FPRs, scalar CSRs (`mstatus`, `mepc`, ...) | TCG plugin reads via `qemu_plugin_read_register()` | DM Abstract Commands | DM Abstract Commands | gdbstub `G` / `P` packets | | V scalar CSRs - `vtype`, `vl` | TCG plugin | DM Program Buffer running `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` | DM Abstract Commands | gdbstub `P` packets | | V scalar CSRs - `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, `mstatus.VS` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Commands | DM Abstract Commands | gdbstub `P` packets | | V regfile `v0..v31` (up to 2 KB) | TCG plugin | scratch region in shared mmap; fixture executes `vle8.v` | fixture executes `vse8.v`; vsim reads RAM bytes | gdbstub `P` packets | | Guest memory | not copied | not copied | not copied | not copied | Memory does not appear in the wire struct because shared mmap removes it from the transport problem. ## Design Rationale **One packed struct is the wire format.** Three components in three languages (QEMU plugin in C, vsim in C++, gdbstub-translation layer in C++) need to agree byte-for-byte on what state means. A C struct in a shared header (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`) is the simplest contract that compiles into all three. Versioning is via `magic` + `version`, with `reserved` bytes for forward growth. **QEMU 1 -> vsim is file-based, vsim -> QEMU 2 is gdbstub-based.** QEMU mainline is used **as-is** (no patches, no fork). It exposes two relevant extension points: the TCG plugin API can *read* state during execution, and gdbstub `-S` can *write* state before the first fetch. Plugins cannot inject state at startup, and there is no `--load-state-from-file` flag. Each direction picks the only working primitive: the plugin drains to a file out of QEMU 1, and gdbstub `G`/`P` packets seed QEMU 2. The vsim side translates between the file and the RSP packets - QEMU never has to know about `hybrid_state_v1`. **Most registers go through DM Abstract Commands; `vtype` and `vl` need the Program Buffer.** GPRs, FPRs, and ordinary CSRs all have abstract-command slots in the AX45MPV Debug Module - one DMI transaction each. `vtype` is the exception: the V spec marks it read-only via direct CSR write because writing it sets the `vill` flag and invalidates the vector unit. The only legal restore for `vtype` + `vl` together is the `vsetvl` instruction, which has to *execute on the hart*. The Debug Module's Program Buffer is exactly that slot: stage one instruction, run it on the halted core, then continue with abstract commands for the rest. **The V regfile rides shared mmap, not the Debug Module.** The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command slots for `v0..v31`; it leaves V to optional Program Buffer or DPI extensions, and the AX45MPV wrapper does not implement either. Pushing 32 × 64-byte regs through the Program Buffer one element at a time would be both slow and intricate. Since both simulators already share an mmap, we reuse it: vsim copies `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`) before `dret`, and the fixture's vsim-phase prologue issues `vle8.v` from that region to load the real V regfile. Drain is the mirror: the fixture's epilogue issues `vse8.v` to scratch before the exit trigger, and vsim memcpy's those bytes into `state.vreg[]`. The fixture is aware of the contract; the V regfile reuses the mmap that already exists for general memory, with no new transport channel. **Into QEMU 2, the V regfile reaches the target through gdbstub `P` packets.** Gdbstub already speaks RV-V via the standard XML target description, so writing `v0..v31` is just one `P` packet per V-reg index after the `G` packet for GPRs. No new mechanism is needed on the QEMU 2 side. ## Architecture Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISK["Shared backing files"] SHM[("/dev/shm RAM
(mmap shared)
+ V scratch
at 0x80FF0000")] STATE_Q["state_qemu.bin
hybrid_state_v1"] STATE_V["state_vsim.bin
hybrid_state_v1"] end subgraph QEMU1["QEMU 1 (functional, MHz)"] Q1_TCG["TCG instruction loop"] Q1_PLUGIN["hybrid_handoff.so
watches csrwi 0x7C0,0"] Q1_TCG --> Q1_PLUGIN end subgraph VSIM["vsim (cycle-accurate, KHz)"] V_PIPE["RTL pipeline
(Verilator + SystemC)"] V_DM["JTAG DM driver (in-process)
Abstract Cmds + Program Buffer"] V_TRIG["csr_trigger
watches csrwi 0x7C0,1"] V_DM -.GPR/FPR/CSR + vsetvl.-> V_PIPE V_PIPE -.retire signals.-> V_TRIG end subgraph QEMU2["QEMU 2 (functional, MHz)"] Q2_GDB["gdbstub -gdb tcp::PORT -S"] Q2_TCG["TCG instruction loop"] Q2_GDB -.G + P packets.-> Q2_TCG end Q1_PLUGIN -- "drain GPR/FPR/CSR/V" --> STATE_Q Q1_PLUGIN -- "qemu_plugin_request_exit" --> VSIM STATE_Q -- "--resume-from-qemu" --> V_DM V_DM <-.V regs via mmap.-> SHM V_TRIG -- "drain (incl. V from scratch)" --> STATE_V V_TRIG -- "exit code 200" --> QEMU2 STATE_V -- "RSP G + P packets" --> Q2_GDB Q1_TCG <-->|"every load/store"| SHM V_PIPE <-->|"every load/store"| SHM Q2_TCG <-->|"every load/store"| SHM ``` ## Walk-Through: `rt_c_v_regs` E2E Test The canonical demo: it exercises **memory** transport (via shared mmap), **GPR / scalar CSR** transport (via the state file), and **V regfile** transport (via the V-scratch trick) all in one round-trip. Source: `hybrid_vsim/hybrid/test/rt_c_v_regs.c`. Two memory regions live in the shared mmap: - `SEED` at `0x80100000` - 16 int32 elements, the input pattern. - `V_SCRATCH` at `0x80FF0000` - 2 KB region, the V regfile transport channel. ### What each phase does ``` Phase 1 (QEMU 1) Phase 2 (vsim) Phase 3 (QEMU 2) ----------------- --------------------------------- -------------------- seed[0..15] = pattern vle32.v v8..v15, (seed) vse32.v v8..v15, (out) csrwi 0x7C0, 0 vadd.vi v9, v9, +100 verify out matches vse8.v v0..v31, (V_SCRATCH) seed (with v9 +100) csrwi 0x7C0, 1 SEMIHOST_EXIT 0 ``` ### State that crosses each handoff | Boundary | Memory state | GPR / scalar CSR state | V regfile state | V scalar CSR state | |--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------| | QEMU 1 -> vsim | `SEED` already filled by Phase 1; vsim sees it via mmap | drained by plugin, restored by DM Abstract Commands | unused by Phase 1 (zero on the wire) | `mstatus.VS` re-enabled, `vsetvl` staged via Program Buffer | | vsim -> QEMU 2 | `SEED` unchanged; # How the Hybrid Simulator Works A friendly tour for engineers new to this codebase. ## The Problem in One Paragraph A pure RTL simulator (`vsim`, built on Verilator + SystemC) is **cycle-accurate**: it tells you exactly how many cycles every instruction takes inside the CPU pipeline. The price is speed: it runs at ~5-9 KHz. Booting Linux, running libc init, and warming up before reaching the actual code you care about can take hours. Meanwhile, QEMU runs the same program **functionally** at MHz speed, but it has no notion of pipeline cycles. The hybrid simulator combines them: run the boring parts in QEMU, switch to vsim for the small region of interest (ROI) you actually want to measure, and (optionally) hand back to QEMU to finish. ## High-Level Concept Four big ideas make this possible: 1. **Memory is shared, not copied.** Both simulators `mmap` the same backing file (`/dev/shm/...`) as their RAM. When QEMU writes a byte at `0x80001000`, vsim sees it instantly, and vice versa. RAM size becomes irrelevant - no transfer cost. 2. **Architectural CPU state is small enough to copy through one packed struct.** GPRs, FPRs, M-mode CSRs, the V regfile (`v0..v31`), and V scalar CSRs (`vtype`, `vl`, `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`) are all described by `struct hybrid_state_v1` in `include/hybrid/state_abi.h`. That struct is the wire contract every component agrees on, written to a file at each handoff. 3. **The trigger is a magic CSR write.** `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` = "QEMU, hand off to vsim". `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` = "vsim, hand back to QEMU". CSR `0x7C0` lives in the user-defined range so it cannot collide with anything real. 4. **Microarchitectural state is thrown away.** Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer - none of it transfers. We accept cold caches at handoff and rely on a "warm-up window" before recording measurements. ## State Categories and Their Transport The wire struct is uniform, but each category travels through a different host primitive depending on what QEMU and the RTL Debug Module are willing to expose. | Category | Out of QEMU 1 | Into vsim | Out of vsim | Into QEMU 2 | |---------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------| | GPRs, FPRs, scalar CSRs (`mstatus`, `mepc`, ...) | TCG plugin reads via `qemu_plugin_read_register()` | DM Abstract Commands | DM Abstract Commands | gdbstub `G` / `P` packets | | V scalar CSRs - `vtype`, `vl` | TCG plugin | DM Program Buffer running `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` | DM Abstract Commands | gdbstub `P` packets | | V scalar CSRs - `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, `mstatus.VS` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Commands | DM Abstract Commands | gdbstub `P` packets | | V regfile `v0..v31` (up to 2 KB) | TCG plugin | scratch region in shared mmap; fixture executes `vle8.v` | fixture executes `vse8.v`; vsim reads RAM bytes | gdbstub `P` packets | | Guest memory | not copied | not copied | not copied | not copied | Memory does not appear in the wire struct because shared mmap removes it from the transport problem. ## Design Rationale **One packed struct is the wire format.** Three components in three languages (QEMU plugin in C, vsim in C++, gdbstub-translation layer in C++) need to agree byte-for-byte on what state means. A C struct in a shared header (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`) is the simplest contract that compiles into all three. Versioning is via `magic` + `version`, with `reserved` bytes for forward growth. **QEMU 1 -> vsim is file-based, vsim -> QEMU 2 is gdbstub-based.** QEMU mainline is used **as-is** (no patches, no fork). It exposes two relevant extension points: the TCG plugin API can *read* state during execution, and gdbstub `-S` can *write* state before the first fetch. Plugins cannot inject state at startup, and there is no `--load-state-from-file` flag. Each direction picks the only working primitive: the plugin drains to a file out of QEMU 1, and gdbstub `G`/`P` packets seed QEMU 2. The vsim side translates between the file and the RSP packets - QEMU never has to know about `hybrid_state_v1`. **Most registers go through DM Abstract Commands; `vtype` and `vl` need the Program Buffer.** GPRs, FPRs, and ordinary CSRs all have abstract-command slots in the AX45MPV Debug Module - one DMI transaction each. `vtype` is the exception: the V spec marks it read-only via direct CSR write because writing it sets the `vill` flag and invalidates the vector unit. The only legal restore for `vtype` + `vl` together is the `vsetvl` instruction, which has to *execute on the hart*. The Debug Module's Program Buffer is exactly that slot: stage one instruction, run it on the halted core, then continue with abstract commands for the rest. **The V regfile rides shared mmap, not the Debug Module.** The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command slots for `v0..v31`; it leaves V to optional Program Buffer or DPI extensions, and the AX45MPV wrapper does not implement either. Pushing 32 × 64-byte regs through the Program Buffer one element at a time would be both slow and intricate. Since both simulators already share an mmap, we reuse it: vsim copies `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`) before `dret`, and the fixture's vsim-phase prologue issues `vle8.v` from that region to load the real V regfile. Drain is the mirror: the fixture's epilogue issues `vse8.v` to scratch before the exit trigger, and vsim memcpy's those bytes into `state.vreg[]`. The fixture is aware of the contract; the V regfile reuses the mmap that already exists for general memory, with no new transport channel. **Into QEMU 2, the V regfile reaches the target through gdbstub `P` packets.** Gdbstub already speaks RV-V via the standard XML target description, so writing `v0..v31` is just one `P` packet per V-reg index after the `G` packet for GPRs. No new mechanism is needed on the QEMU 2 side. ## Architecture Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISK["Shared backing files"] SHM[("/dev/shm RAM

-| Phase B `verify_a` loop passes | **Memory written in QEMU survives into vsim.** Shared mmap works as advertised. |

-| vsim runs the XOR loop | Resumed register state was correct - in particular `t0`, `t1`, `t3`, `t4` are reinitialized by Phase B but the **PC** must have been restored to point at Phase B's first instruction. |
-| Phase C `verify_c` loop passes | **Memory written in vsim survives into QEMU 2.** Both transfer paths work. |
-| Final exit code 0 | Full round-trip succeeded. Exit 50 means QEMU->vsim memory broke; exit 51 means vsim->QEMU memory broke. |
+| Phase 1 fills `SEED` via int32 stores      | QEMU 1's RAM backend is the shared file (not a private anonymous mapping).                     |
+| `vle32.v v8..v15, (seed)` runs in vsim     | **Memory written in QEMU survives into vsim**, AND `mstatus.VS` + `vtype/vl` were correctly re-applied (otherwise `vsetvli` would have trapped). |

+| Phase 3 `vse32.v` + verify pass            | **V regfile written in vsim survives into QEMU 2.** The `V_SCRATCH` -> `state.vreg[]` -> gdbstub `P` chain works. `v9 == seed + 100` confirms the *mutated* value, not zero-init. |
+| Final exit code 0                          | Full round-trip succeeded for memory, GPRs, V regfile, and V-CSRs.                             |
-If any link breaks, the distinct exit codes tell you exactly which transfer path failed.

+If `vle32` traps, `mstatus.VS` is wrong. If the regfile verification fails for `v9` only, the V scratch path lost the mutation. If GPRs are wrong, `t0` (holding the SEED address) was not restored before the asm executed.
-## Why the Asymmetry? File for QEMU->vsim, gdbstub for vsim->QEMU
-A natural question on first reading: vsim reads register state from a file, but QEMU 2 receives it through gdbstub. Why not use a file in both directions?
-**Short answer**: QEMU has no built-in "load architectural state from a file before fetch starts" hook. gdbstub is exactly that hook, already shipping in mainline QEMU.

-The whole design rests on one rule (`hybrid_plan.md` Section 2.4): **no QEMU patches, no fork**. Mainline QEMU 9.2.0 is used as-is. That ships exactly two extension points relevant to state injection:
-| Extension point | What it can do |

-| TCG plugin API (`qemu_plugin_*`) | **Read** registers and memory while QEMU runs. `qemu_plugin_read_register()` exists and is reliable for all reg classes. There is no clean way to set all 32 GPRs + PC + CSRs *before the very first TB executes* - plugins are designed for instrumentation, not state injection at startup. |

-| gdbstub (`-gdb tcp::PORT -S`) | Stops QEMU before any guest instruction runs and opens a port speaking the standard RSP protocol. Whoever connects can write all registers, then resume. This is exactly the "set state, then start" primitive we need. |
-So each direction picks the only working mechanism mainline QEMU offers:
-                 vsim has the JTAG DM already, drives it in-process to WRITE
-                 regs into RTL via Abstract Commands.
-vsim -> QEMU 2:  vsim drains regs via JTAG DM into a state file.
-                 vsim connects, sends 'G' packet (regs from the file), 'c'.
-                 QEMU does not parse the file - vsim translates file -> RSP packet.
-1. **No CLI flag exists.** `qemu-system-riscv64` has no `--load-state-from <file>` for an arbitrary register struct. Adding one means patching QEMU - ruled out.

-2. **Plugins can't do it either.** A plugin's earliest hook (`vcpu_init`, first TB callback) runs *after* the reset vector executes some instructions. Setting the PC from a plugin at that point fights QEMU's fetch loop.

-3. **gdbstub is purpose-built for this.** `-S` (start halted) plus the RSP `G` packet are the canonical way to externally seed QEMU state. Mature, covers every reg class the gdb XML exposes, works for every machine type.
-4. **Decouples the wire format.** vsim translates `struct hybrid_state_v1` into RSP packets. QEMU 2 only sees a standard gdb client. No coupling between QEMU and the project's struct definition.

-Memory does not face this problem because QEMU **does** have a file-backed memory primitive (`-object memory-backend-file,share=on`), so the same mmap trick works on both QEMU instances. Registers don't have an equivalent "memory-backend-file for regs" - gdbstub is the closest thing, and it happens to be enough.

-**RSP** = Remote Serial Protocol, the text-based wire protocol gdb uses to talk to a remote target (gdbserver, QEMU's gdbstub, OpenOCD, J-Link, etc.). Every packet has the form `$<payload>#<checksum>`.

-**The `G` packet** is one specific RSP command: *"write all CPU registers at once."* Its payload is a single hex string concatenating every register's bytes in the exact order the target's gdb XML description declares them.
-| `g` (lowercase) | read all registers - target replies with the hex blob |
-| `G` (uppercase) | write all registers - sender provides the hex blob |
-| `p N` / `P N=...` | read / write a single register by index |
-For RV64 with FP and CSRs, the `G` payload looks like (hex, little-endian per register):
-Concretely, what vsim does in the handback (see `hybrid_vsim/src/hybrid/gdbstub_session.hpp` and `gdbstub_client.hpp`):
-   (QEMU opens PORT, halts before any insn fetch)
-2. connect TCP, do gdb handshake (qSupported, qXfer:features:read, ...)
-3. build the hex string from struct hybrid_state_v1:
-5. send  $c#<cksum>                   -> QEMU starts executing at state.pc

-That's the entire protocol exchange. No QEMU code changes; no custom file format on the QEMU side; just standard gdb RSP. Reference: GDB Remote Serial Protocol, sections "Packets" and "Register Packet Format" in the gdb manual.
-- `include/hybrid/state_abi.h` - the wire contract; both QEMU plugin and vsim include this exact header.
-- `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` - the QEMU side. Look at `resolve_handles` and the entry-trigger callback.
-- `hybrid_vsim/hybrid_plan.md` - the original design document, with full rationale for every decision.
+- `qemu_plugin/hybrid_handoff.c` - QEMU drain side. See `resolve_handles` and the entry-trigger callback.
- `run_e2e.sh` - run all e2e tests locally without going through CMake/CTest.

content.content:
1	# Hybrid Simulator: QEMU + Verilator (vsim)
2	
3	A plan to extend `vsim` from a pure Verilator cycle-accurate simulator into a hybrid functional/RTL simulator that runs the boring code in QEMU at MHz speed and switches into Verilator only for the cycle-accurate region of interest (ROI), then optionally hands back.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Context
8	
9	`vsim` runs at 5-9 KHz on Andes RISC-V cores. A typical workload spends >99% of cycles in boot, libc init, and warm-up before reaching the kernel under study. Today the only escape hatch is `--save`/`--continue`, which still requires an initial RTL run to reach the snapshot point. There is also already a `--kanata-loop-{start,end,skip,record}` mechanism (`src/main.cpp:183-214`) that identifies the interesting loop iterations.
10	
11	We want: run everything before the ROI in QEMU (functional, MHz), hand the architectural state to Verilator at the ROI boundary, run the ROI cycle-accurately to record kanata/clarity pipeline data, and optionally hand back to QEMU to finish.
12	
13	The intended outcome: 100x-1000x reduction in wall-clock time for benchmarking workflows that only care about steady-state pipeline behavior of a small kernel, with byte-identical kanata output for the recorded window vs. a fully-RTL baseline.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## 2. Tutorial: Why This Is Technically Feasible
18	
19	### 2.1 The state-exchange problem, reframed
20	
21	A naive hybrid sim has to "transfer" CPU state between two simulators. In practice the state splits into three categories with very different costs:
22	
23	| Category | Items | Size | Strategy |
24	|---|---|---|---|
25	| Architectural regs | x0..x31, f0..f31, V regs, key CSRs | 4-8 KB | **Copy** via well-defined ABI struct |
26	| Memory | guest RAM, ILM/DLM | MB-GB | **Share** via `mmap` - zero copy |
27	| Microarchitectural state | TLB, caches, BPU, store buffer | KB-MB | **Discard** - let cold starts repopulate |
28	
29	The key trick: **memory does not move**. QEMU runs with `-object memory-backend-file,share=on,mem-path=/dev/shm/vsim-ram`. vsim's `SimpleMemory::data` (`src/platform/simple_memory.hpp:20`) is changed to an `mmap` of the same fd. Both simulators read and write the same physical bytes. The same trick is used by QEMU's CPR (`docs/devel/migration/CPR.rst`) and gem5's `SimpleSwitchableProcessor`. RAM size becomes irrelevant.
30	
31	Microarch state is intentionally discarded. We accept cold caches/BPU on entry into Verilator and rely on the existing `kanata_loop_skip N` mechanism to warm them up before recording. This matches the gem5 "switch + warmup" pattern.
32	
33	### 2.2 The trigger: a magic CSR convention
34	
35	We need both sides to agree on "the ROI starts here." Reuse the user-defined CSR space at `0x7C0`:
36	
37	```asm
38	csrwi 0x7C0, 0   # enter detail (QEMU -> vsim)
39	csrwi 0x7C0, 1   # exit detail  (vsim -> QEMU)
40	```
41	
42	- **QEMU** sees this via a TCG plugin: `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb()` with a per-instruction filter that pattern-matches the encoding of `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` (mask `0xfff03073` against the 32-bit insn). On hit, the plugin drains state and exits.
43	- **vsim** sees this via the existing retire signal taps in `src/signals/common_signals_45.hpp`. We pre-scan the ELF for PCs containing `csrw 0x7C0, *` (LIEF is already linked - `src/utils/elf_loader.hpp`), then watch for retire-of-PC against that set.
44	
45	Phase 4 lifts this further: `--kanata-loop-start <pc>` and `--kanata-loop-end <pc>` already specify the ROI by PC. The orchestrator wraps these as PC-trigger events and **the binary needs no magic CSR at all**.
46	
47	We deliberately avoid `tohost`/`fromhost` (HTIF reserved) and `ecall` (already used by the runtime).
48	
49	### 2.3 The write-back path: existing JTAG/DTM, in-process
50	
51	This is the highest-leverage finding. vsim already has:
52	
53	- `src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp` - a full JTAG DTM implementation with the RISC-V Debug Module
54	- `--jtag-port` CLI flag and OpenOCD support (`src/main.cpp:148-154`)
55	
56	The RISC-V Debug Spec gives Abstract Commands that read/write GPRs (`regno` 0x1000-0x101F), FPRs (0x1020-0x103F), and CSRs (0x0000-0x0FFF) directly via the Debug Module. **Writing all 32 GPRs + 32 FPRs + ~20 critical CSRs is ~85 DMI transactions**, which is ~hundreds of cycles - negligible compared to the millions of cycles we save.
57	
58	We drive the DTM **in-process**, calling its `handle_dmi_*` methods directly without an OpenOCD socket. No DPI shim, no Verilog modifications, no per-CPU-variant code (works for all ten CPU wrappers in `src/cpu_wrappers/`).
59	
60	The sequence to "resume from QEMU state":
61	
62	1. Reset CPU; halt request via DM
63	2. For each register in the ABI struct, issue Abstract Command (`regno` + data)
64	3. Set DPC = saved PC
65	4. Issue `dret` via Program Buffer
66	
67	Vector registers and any CPU-specific custom CSR that DM doesn't expose are deferred to Phase 5. If DM coverage is incomplete we add a per-CPU Program Buffer fallback or, last resort, an mmap-shared V regfile via DPI.
68	
69	### 2.4 The QEMU side: a TCG plugin against the existing build
70	
71	`/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64` is QEMU **9.2.0** (verified). This is recent enough for the full plugin API:
72	
73	- `qemu_plugin_register_vcpu_insn_exec_cb()` - per-insn callback
74	- `qemu_plugin_get_registers()` / `qemu_plugin_read_register()` - drain GPR/FP/CSR
75	- `qemu_plugin_request_exit()` - stop QEMU cleanly
76	
77	Templates already on disk: `/local/nick/qemu_v5/contrib/plugins/{execlog.c, hotblocks.c, bbv.c}`. We add one new plugin `hybrid_handoff.c` next to them. No QEMU patches, no fork.
78	
79	### 2.5 What does NOT work and is out of scope
80	
81	- **MMIO inside the ROI**: device state lives in QEMU C and has no mirror in RTL. Document; fail loudly via an interconnect watch in Phase 5.
82	- **lr/sc reservations across handoff**: invalidate at handoff; do not transfer.
83	- **Self-modifying code in the ROI**: the magic CSR is a synchronization boundary by convention.
84	- **mcycle/minstret continuity**: monotonic across handoffs but not equal to a fully-RTL run; documented discontinuity.
85	- **Andes-internal QEMU fork** (`andescycle-dev-v0.16-17-g03dfe23.tar.gz`): treated as opaque. Plan against mainline QEMU 9.2.
86

89	## 3. Architecture at a Glance
90	
91	```
92	                              shared mmap RAM (/dev/shm/vsim-ram)
93	                                           |
94	              +----------------------------+----------------------------+
95	              |                                                         |
96	              v                                                         v
97	       QEMU 9.2.0 + plugin                                    vsim (SystemC + Verilator)
98	       +---------------------+                                +-----------------------+
99	       | TCG insn loop       |     state.bin (ABI struct)     | SystemC main loop     |
100	       | hybrid_handoff.so   |  <-->  [ABI v1 packed]   <-->  | jtag_dtm in-process   |
101	       | csrw 0x7C0 -> drain |                                | csr_trigger -> drain  |
102	       +---------------------+                                +-----------------------+
103	              ^                                                         ^
104	              |                                                         |
105	              +--orchestrator script (vsim-hybrid)----------------------+
106	                  enter-pc / exit-pc mode also supported
107	```
108	
109	State exchange contract: a single packed `struct hybrid_state_v1` (header + GPR + FPR + CSR + V) defined in `hybrid/include/state_abi.h`, written atomically (`O_TMPFILE` + `linkat`) at handoff.

113	## 4. Design Decisions (locked)
114	
115	| # | Decision | Rationale |
116	|---|---|---|
117	| a | **JTAG/DTM in-process** for register write-back (vs. DPI shim or synthetic insn stream) | Existing infrastructure (`src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp`); zero RTL changes; works across all 10 CPU wrappers |
118	| b | Same for CSRs; Program Buffer fallback for unreachable ones | Same |
119	| c | **Modify `SimpleMemory` in-place**: backing becomes `std::span<uint8_t>` with optional mmap; existing `std::vector` path retained when mmap unset | All call sites use `.data()`/`.size()` only - both span and vector satisfy. Templated class used as concrete member; forking is worse |
120	| d | **TCG plugin** (in-process, fastest) against existing QEMU 9.2.0 build at `/local/nick/qemu_v5` | Plugin API is feature-complete in 9.x; templates available; no fork |
121	| e | **Packed C struct** in shared file (vs. protobuf/JSON) | C-and-C++-compatible single header; zero parse cost; static_assert offsets to catch drift |
