It exposes two relevant extension points: the TCG plugin API can *read* state during execution, and gdbstub `-S` can *write* state before the first fetch. Plugins cannot inject state at startup, and there is no `--load-state-from-file` flag. Each direction picks the only working primitive: the plugin drains to a file out of QEMU 1, and gdbstub `G`/`P` packets seed QEMU 2. The vsim side translates between the file and the RSP packets - QEMU never has to know about `hybrid_state_v1`. **Passthrough is the largest performance lever.** A naive design would round-trip every field of `hybrid_state_v1` through DM Abstract Commands - hundreds of DMI transactions. Instead, `HandoffController::set_passthrough_input(snapshot)` keeps QEMU 1's drained state in vsim memory; when the exit trigger fires, `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough(snapshot)` re-reads only x1..x31 + PC + (when V) the six V CSRs via DM, and merges those over the snapshot. Everything the ROI is *not allowed* to touch (FPRs, M-mode CSRs except mtvec, PMP) flows from QEMU 1 straight into the QEMU 2 gdbstub restore. ROI-mutates-FP would silently lose those changes - that's an explicit, documented limitation, not an oversight. **The Debug Module handles most ISA registers; `vtype`/`vl` need the Program Buffer.** GPRs, FPRs, and ordinary CSRs all have abstract-command slots in the AX45MPV DM - one DMI transaction each. `vtype` is the exception: the V spec marks it read-only via direct CSR write because writing it sets the `vill` flag and invalidates the vector unit. The only legal restore for `vtype` + `vl` together is the `vsetvl` instruction, which has to *execute on the hart*. The Program Buffer is exactly that slot: stage `vl` and `vtype` in scratch GPRs (x5/x6), then execute `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` in the PB. The GPR loop afterwards overwrites x5/x6, so no leakage. **Tier-A V CSRs use Abstract-first with a Program-Buffer fallback.** `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, and `mstatus.VS` are spec-compliant to direct CSR write, but the AX45MPV DM may reject the abstract path on a given build. `ResumeDriver::write_csr_abstract_or_pb` tries Abstract first, checks `abstractcs.cmderr`, and on failure clears the sticky error and falls back to a one-instruction PB sequence (`csrrw x0, csr, x5`). The fallback is silent and per-CSR, so a partially-implemented DM does not block the rest of the restore. **The V regfile rides shared mmap, not the Debug Module.** The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command slots for `v0..v31`; it leaves V to optional Program Buffer or DPI extensions, and the AX45MPV wrapper does not implement either. Pushing 32 x 64-byte regs through the Program Buffer one element at a time would be both slow and intricate. Since both simulators already share an mmap, we reuse it: vsim copies `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region before `dret`, and the fixture's vsim-phase prologue issues `vle8.v` from that region to load the real V regfile. Drain mirrors: the fixture's epilogue issues `vse8.v` to scratch, and the simulator wrapper reads those bytes back into `state.vreg[]` after `StateDrain` returns. The fixture is aware of the contract; the V regfile reuses the mmap that already exists for general memory. **Missing-register tolerance is part of the contract.** A given QEMU build may not advertise every PMP slot, and the DM may not implement every CSR. `apply_state` in `qemu_handback.hpp` silently skips any name not in the discovered RegMap (`try_write_named`); `StateDrain::read_64bit_register` returns 0 and clears `cmderr` when the DM rejects. Failing closed would block bring-up on every QEMU/RTL revision; failing soft keeps the happy path moving and surfaces real bugs at the fixture level. ## Architecture Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DISK["Shared backing files"] SHM[("/dev/shm RAM

# How the Hybrid Simulator Works A friendly tour for engineers new to this codebase. ## The Problem in One Paragraph A pure RTL simulator (`vsim`, built on Verilator + SystemC) is **cycle-accurate**: it tells you exactly how many cycles every instruction takes inside the CPU pipeline. The price is speed: it runs at ~5-9 KHz. Booting Linux, running libc init, and warming up before reaching the actual code you care about can take hours. Meanwhile, QEMU runs the same program **functionally** at MHz speed, but it has no notion of pipeline cycles. The hybrid simulator combines them: run the boring parts in QEMU, switch to vsim for the small region of interest (ROI) you actually want to measure, and (optionally) hand back to QEMU to finish. ## High-Level Concept Five big ideas make this possible: 1. **Memory is shared, not copied.** Both simulators `mmap` the same backing file (`/dev/shm/...`) as their RAM. When QEMU writes a byte at `0x80001000`, vsim sees it instantly, and vice versa. RAM size becomes irrelevant. 2. **All architectural state fits in one packed struct.** GPRs, FPRs, M-mode CSRs (mstatus, mepc, mtvec, ...), PMP (16 cfg + 64 addr), V scalar CSRs (vtype, vl, vstart, vxsat, vxrm, vcsr, vlenb), and the full V regfile (`v0..v31`, up to 2 KB) all live in `struct hybrid_state_v1` (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`). That struct is the wire contract every component agrees on. 3. **vsim only refreshes what the ROI can mutate.** vsim restores into the hart only what it needs to run (GPRs + PC + V state); on the way out it drains back only what could have changed (GPRs + PC + V state). Everything else (FP, M-mode CSRs, PMP, V regfile bytes when the ROI doesn't touch them) **passes through unchanged** from the QEMU 1 snapshot to the QEMU 2 restore. This skips ~85+ DMI transactions per handoff. 4. **The trigger is a magic CSR write or a PC match.** `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` enters vsim; `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` exits it. CSR `0x7C0` is in the user-defined range. A third form, `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1`, decides at runtime based on `rs1`'s value (1 = exit). PC-trigger mode skips the magic CSR entirely - the orchestrator names entry/exit PCs on the CLI. 5. **Microarchitectural state is thrown away.** Caches, TLB, branch predictor, store buffer - none of it transfers. We accept cold caches at handoff and rely on a "warm-up window" before recording. ## State Categories and Their Transport The wire struct is uniform; transport per category depends on what QEMU's plugin API and the AX45MPV Debug Module are willing to expose. | Category | QEMU 1 -> snapshot | snapshot -> vsim hart | vsim hart -> snapshot | snapshot -> QEMU 2 | |---------------------------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|--------------------| | GPRs `x1..x31`, PC | TCG plugin reads | DM Abstract Commands (DPC for PC) | DM Abstract Commands (fresh read) | gdbstub `G` packet | | `mtvec` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Command | passthrough (not re-read) | gdbstub `P` | | Other M-mode CSRs (`mstatus`, `mepc`, `mscratch`, `medeleg`, `mideleg`, `mip`, `mie`, `mcycle`, `minstret`, `mcause`, `mtval`, `satp`) | TCG plugin | passthrough (not written) | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (per `kCsrByName`) | | FPRs `f0..f31` | TCG plugin | passthrough | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (per `hybrid_fpr_names`) | | PMP (16 cfg + 64 addr) | TCG plugin | passthrough | passthrough | gdbstub `P` (each slot) | | V scalar CSRs - `vtype`, `vl` | TCG plugin | DM Program Buffer running `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` | DM Abstract Command (fresh read) | gdbstub `P` | | V scalar CSRs - `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, `mstatus.VS` | TCG plugin | DM Abstract Command, **with Program-Buffer fallback** if the DM rejects | DM Abstract Command (fresh read) | gdbstub `P` | | V regfile `v0..v31` (up to 2 KB) | TCG plugin | shared-mmap scratch region (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`); fixture executes `vle8.v` to load | fixture executes `vse8.v` back to scratch; vsim memcpys those bytes into `state.vreg[]` | gdbstub `P` (per V-reg index) | | `vlenb` | TCG plugin | not restored (read-only, fixed by NDS_VLEN) | passthrough (verified, not re-read) | not sent | | Guest memory | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | -- (in shared mmap) | "Passthrough" means: vsim copies the field from the inbound snapshot into the outbound snapshot unchanged. The hart never sees it; the field rides from QEMU 1 directly to QEMU 2. ## Trigger Modes Two ways to delimit the ROI; pick at runtime via `TRIGGER_MODE=csr|pc`. | Mode | Entry into vsim | Exit out of vsim | Source of truth | |------|------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------| | CSR | `csrwi 0x7C0, 0` (or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with `rs1==0`) | `csrwi 0x7C0, 1` (or `csrrw rd, 0x7C0, rs1` with `rs1==1`) | the binary itself | | PC | plugin arg `enter_pc=0x...` (no insn match) | vsim CLI `--hybrid-exit-pc 0x...` (synthetic hit added via `CsrTrigger::add_pc_hit`) | the orchestrator/CLI; binary is unchanged | Three handoff kinds (`hybrid_handoff_kind` in `include/hybrid/insn_match.h`): - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_ENTER` - QEMU drains and exits; vsim ignores at retire. - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_EXIT` - vsim drains and exits; QEMU does not encounter this PC. - `HYBRID_HANDOFF_RUNTIME` - vsim decodes `rs1` from the matched insn, reads its current GPR value, fires only when it equals 1. -**One packed struct is the wire format.** Three components in three languages (QEMU plugin in C, vsim in C++, gdbstub-translation layer in C++) need to agree byte-for-byte on what state means. +**One packed struct is the wire format.** Three components in three languages (QEMU plugin in C, vsim in C++, gdbstub-translation layer in C++) need to agree byte-for-byte on what state means. A C struct in a shared header (`include/hybrid/state_abi.h`) is the simplest contract that compiles into all three. Versioning is via `magic` + `version`, with `reserved` bytes for forward growth (vxsat/vxrm/vcsr were added by consuming `reserved` rather than bumping to v2).

**QEMU 1 -> vsim is file-based, vsim -> QEMU 2 is gdbstub-based.** QEMU mainline is used **as-is** (no patches, no fork). It exposes two relevant extension points: the TCG plugin API can *read* state during execution, and gdbstub `-S` can *write* state before the first fetch. Plugins cannot inject state at startup, and there is no `--load-state-from-file` flag. Each direction picks the only working primitive: the plugin drains to a file out of QEMU 1, and gdbstub `G`/`P` packets seed QEMU 2. The vsim side translates between the file and the RSP packets - QEMU never has to know about `hybrid_state_v1`.

-**Most registers go through DM Abstract Commands; `vtype` and `vl` need the Program Buffer.** GPRs, FPRs, and ordinary CSRs all have abstract-command slots in the AX45MPV Debug Module - one DMI transaction each. `vtype` is the exception: the V spec marks it read-only via direct CSR write because writing it sets the `vill` flag and invalidates the vector unit. The only legal restore for `vtype` + `vl` together is the `vsetvl` instruction, which has to *execute on the hart*. The Debug Module's Program Buffer is exactly that slot: stage one instruction, run it on the halted core, then continue with abstract commands for the rest.

+**Passthrough is the largest performance lever.** A naive design would round-trip every field of `hybrid_state_v1` through DM Abstract Commands - hundreds of DMI transactions. Instead, `HandoffController::set_passthrough_input(snapshot)` keeps QEMU 1's drained state in vsim memory; when the exit trigger fires, `StateDrain::drain_with_passthrough(snapshot)` re-reads only x1..x31 + PC + (when V) the six V CSRs via DM, and merges those over the snapshot. Everything the ROI is *not allowed* to touch (FPRs, M-mode CSRs except mtvec, PMP) flows from QEMU 1 straight into the QEMU 2 gdbstub restore. ROI-mutates-FP would silently lose those changes - that's an explicit, documented limitation, not an oversight.

-**The V regfile rides shared mmap, not the Debug Module.** The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command slots for `v0..v31`; it leaves V to optional Program Buffer or DPI extensions, and the AX45MPV wrapper does not implement either. Pushing 32 × 64-byte regs through the Program Buffer one element at a time would be both slow and intricate. Since both simulators already share an mmap, we reuse it: vsim copies `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region (`hybrid::kVScratchGpa = 0x80FF0000`) before `dret`, and the fixture's vsim-phase prologue issues `vle8.v` from that region to load the real V regfile. Drain is the mirror: the fixture's epilogue issues `vse8.v` to scratch before the exit trigger, and vsim memcpy's those bytes into `state.vreg[]`. The fixture is aware of the contract; the V regfile reuses the mmap that already exists for general memory, with no new transport channel.

+**The Debug Module handles most ISA registers; `vtype`/`vl` need the Program Buffer.** GPRs, FPRs, and ordinary CSRs all have abstract-command slots in the AX45MPV DM - one DMI transaction each. `vtype` is the exception: the V spec marks it read-only via direct CSR write because writing it sets the `vill` flag and invalidates the vector unit. The only legal restore for `vtype` + `vl` together is the `vsetvl` instruction, which has to *execute on the hart*. The Program Buffer is exactly that slot: stage `vl` and `vtype` in scratch GPRs (x5/x6), then execute `vsetvl x0, x5, x6` in the PB. The GPR loop afterwards overwrites x5/x6, so no leakage.

-**Into QEMU 2, the V regfile reaches the target through gdbstub `P` packets.** Gdbstub already speaks RV-V via the standard XML target description, so writing `v0..v31` is just one `P` packet per V-reg index after the `G` packet for GPRs. No new mechanism is needed on the QEMU 2 side.

+**Tier-A V CSRs use Abstract-first with a Program-Buffer fallback.** `vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, and `mstatus.VS` are spec-compliant to direct CSR write, but the AX45MPV DM may reject the abstract path on a given build. `ResumeDriver::write_csr_abstract_or_pb` tries Abstract first, checks `abstractcs.cmderr`, and on failure clears the sticky error and falls back to a one-instruction PB sequence (`csrrw x0, csr, x5`). The fallback is silent and per-CSR, so a partially-implemented DM does not block the rest of the restore.

+**The V regfile rides shared mmap, not the Debug Module.** The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command slots for `v0..v31`; it leaves V to optional Program Buffer or DPI extensions, and the AX45MPV wrapper does not implement either. Pushing 32 x 64-byte regs through the Program Buffer one element at a time would be both slow and intricate. Since both simulators already share an mmap, we reuse it: vsim copies `state.vreg[]` into a fixed scratch region before `dret`, and the fixture's vsim-phase prologue issues `vle8.v` from that region to load the real V regfile. Drain mirrors: the fixture's epilogue issues `vse8.v` to scratch, and the simulator wrapper reads those bytes back into `state.vreg[]` after `StateDrain` returns. The fixture is aware of the contract; the V regfile reuses the mmap that already exists for general memory.

+**Missing-register tolerance is part of the contract.** A given QEMU build may not advertise every PMP slot, and the DM may not implement every CSR. `apply_state` in `qemu_handback.hpp` silently skips any name not in the discovered RegMap (`try_write_named`); `StateDrain::read_64bit_register` returns 0 and clears `cmderr` when the DM rejects. Failing closed would block bring-up on every QEMU/RTL revision; failing soft keeps the happy path moving and surfaces real bugs at the fixture level.
